turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cox?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Yes sir?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me look at this precise letter: it states, doesn't it, that "It has come to my attention that your home is being foreclosed on." Couldn't that be misleading? It certainly indicates familiarity with the precise piece of real estate.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Well, I think what it indicates, Justice Blackmun, is that Mr. Shapiro had gone to the court records and determined that, in fact, there was a foreclosure action pending. I don't think that it indicates any more than that. And I think the important thing to note with respect to this particular advertisement is that it was submitted to three different state bodies, and in not one of those three, did anybody say anything about it being false, misleading, deceptive, or anything else. It was submitted to the state advertising commission,who in the first instance, reviews all advertisements. It was submitted to the ethics committee; again, no sta statement that there was any problem; it was submitted to the state supreme court; the state supreme court did not in any way base its decision --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But Mr. Cox, isn't it possible that all three of those agencies, while it's clearly banned by an existing rule, so we don't have to reach the question of whether the particular advertisement is misleading.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: That is a possibility. But I do not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And in fact, has anybody reviewed this particular letter under the rule that was promulgated after the letter was submitted?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: The Kentucky supreme court did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they didn't tell us anything about whether it was misleading or not, did they?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Kentucky supreme court simply said it was banned.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Banned by a broad regulation.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: But they cited -- and I think it's important that the cited in their decision the fact that it had been reviewed by these lower court -- these lower bodies, and there had been no finding that it was false, misleading, or deceptive. I think we can't --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is no finding that way, but there was no finding the other way either.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Cox — go ahead.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Would it not be more neutral if they said, started off by saying, "Is your home being foreclosed?" There again, there would be no connotation of familiarity with this particular thing, and it would be much more like the I.U.D. case that we had some time ago.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: But this letter is sent to people whose homes are being foreclosed on.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well —

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: I mean, that's

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- I mean, that would be a mistake. That's what concerns me a little bit is there is a possibility of it being misleading.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cox, do you think that the state is free to impose higher standards of truthfulness and honesty on advertising like this than would be the case for selling soap or vacuum cleaners, or something of that kind?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: It is my view that the false, misleading, and deceptive rubric has to be judged in context; and we're talking in the context of a letter coming from an attorney, so obviously —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. That's why I'm asking the question. 2 15 that some were saying, "Well, wouldn't pre-submission address and that had been the basis for Kentucky's rule — would you say that the result was still the same?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: No. I don't believe that we should get into the least common denominator situation. I don't concede that doing a survey -- how would you survey about advertisments, written advertisements in general?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You regard it as "nugget," I thought? So I thought perhaps you thought it was useful?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: No. No, I think it is useful on the other side.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I mean, what if it had come out the other way?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: I think if it had come out the other way, then we'd be in a problem. But it says, "virtually no one." So if 99 percent of the Florida people had said they felt intimidated, overreached, and whatever, then maybe we'd have a problem. But I don't believe that's even a remote possibility. I don't think that there's any way that you can design a survey which would survey for the -- to cover all possible written advertisments. I just don't think that's the case. Our view is that, consistent with Zauderer; it's right there in writing, you have to look at each advertisement individually, and we've got a perfect process.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I think that if you take it you think that some targeted mailings could be forbidden?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: If on their face are false, misleading, and deceptive.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes .

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: And some can't. Some cannot be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, if that's the case, what is the justification for declaring this rule invalid on its face? That's what you asked, isn't it?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Well, I think, well, as the first matter I asked that Mr. Shapiro be able to be permitted to send out this letter, but yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, then, all you're really asking is that, as applied to your case, this rule is unconstitutional?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Right. The problem --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that what you said?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's all you ask?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: That's all I can ask, because --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The overbreadth is not applicable in commercial speech?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Right. And that is what has caused the problems in the lawyer advertising area.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cox, the Kentucky court did strike down the original rule, did it not?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It withdrew it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And do I understand — pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It withdrew it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Withdrew it? Did it?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: I thought it struck it down.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, all right.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: I believe it struck it down, although I don't understand the reason.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I was going to ask next, did the Kentucky supreme court say why they struck that one down?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: They said why, but I think they misspoke. They said they were striking it down in view of Zauderer. I think what they really meant was they were striking it down in view of Primus, because the old rule had prohibited targeted direct mail soliciting for both pecuniary and non-pecuniary gain — that's Primus on all fours. In Primus.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the rule you have brought before us now, as I understand it, is a precise counterpart of the ABA rule, is it not?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Yes sir. And it is precisely the same -- it may be more severe than the old Kentucky rule -- the only difference between this rule and the old Kentucky rule, is that the old Kentucky rule, a part of it was unconstitutional under Primus, but as applied to the facts of this case, there is no we can have hearings; and most importantly, this thing can go all the way to the Kentucky supreme court in one jump. If the advertising commission thinks there's a problem here they can apply to the Kentucky supreme court for a restraining order to restrain publication.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cox, how many states today do you think have this flat ban on targeted direct mail?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: It's reported in our brief -- and our numbers differ from the numbers that they reported, but it's around perhaps half. I'm not sure -- we had gotten our figures out of the ABA service on attorney advertising, and so it's a little clearer, but there is a significant number that do have the ban, and I think most of it is due to the fact that it's contained in the ABA rules. So whether it — the question is, is this case like Ohralik or like Zauderer? I think it's clearly like Zauderer and R.M.J., and the other cases. In Ohralik, one of the commentators said, "It's a lot easier to throw out — " or one of the commentators talking about this problem with respect to Ohralik said it would be a lot easier to throw out a letter than it is a 200 pound lawyer sitting on your couch. And I think that's an important distinction. We don't have Mr. Shapiro in their living room, or if we're going to make it like Ohralik, we don't have them in the what's on the face of the letter, that's all we have.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you quoted from Zauderer; said that written advertisement may include legal advice of some kind. But there's no legal advice in this letter.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: No. I beg your pardon. But it does talk about the possibility of bankruptcy. And that would be the legal advice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where does it say anything about bankruptcy? Where would the layman know it said anything about bankruptcy? It says, "Federal law may allow you to keep your home," and so forth. And if I'm a layman, I'm not sure that means bankruptcy.

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: That's correct. But that's the legal advice: federal law may permit you to keep your home. Also, this rule -- prohibiting targeted direct mail solicitation, doesn't make any sense. Take the Zauderer advertisement: we identify people, women, who have this potential problem, and we send that specific advertisement to them, and that's not permitted under this rule. Take another step: if we take the Shapiro letter and we send it to everybody in Hart County, Kentucky, everybody in Hart County, Kentucky, even though there are only five people in Hart County, Kentucky who are interested in getting it, then that's okay. What you're faced with is a situation where Dick 22 kind of information based -- is it better for people to find out about the availability of legal services under those circumstances, or from their next-door-neighbor; or in a bar; or who knows where? We're talking about a situation where we're making legal advice and advertising legal services that are clearly regulated by the bar association. The other alternatives provide information about lawyers under situations which are much less likely to be truthful and non-deceptive.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We certainly don't know in this case that Kentucky supreme court said this targeted solicitation is, has to be, banned under our rule, even though it is not deceptive, and even though it is truthful, and even though it is not misleading?

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: They didn't say that. All they said was that they recognized that that finding that there was no finding to that effect in the administrative agencies below, and then they dropped it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, let's assume a court having read the cases that you brought before them, that no advertising by a lawyer can be banned; no advertising like this can be banned unless it's false, misleading, as long as it 's truthful; and then it just says, "We do not approve this letter."

turns.name: DONALD L. COX
turns.text: Without saying why? touched on briefly by Justice Stevens in a question that he asked. Listening to Petitioner's argument, the Court might conclude that what we had below was an attack on the entire Kentucky advertising process. That is not the case. Justice Stevens asked, "Was the letter reviewed after Rule 7.3 was adopted?" And the answer to that is emphatically, "no." The Kentucky supreme court opinion begins with these words: "This is a petition for review of supreme court rule 3.135, which has been interpreted by the advertising commission to prohibit targeted direct mail advertising by lawyers."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How does this disapprove this letter?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: The Kentucky supreme court did not as such disapprove this letter, Justice White.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why it disapproved it?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: It did neither. What it said was that Rule 3.135 pertaining to advertising, was too broad. What Petitioner asked below was that Rule 3.135 be deleted; and what Kentucky did in referring to Zauderer three times in the opinion of the court, was specifically said, "It is the decision of this court that supreme court rule 3.135 be deleted." That is what they did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you think that Shapiro was free to go ahead with his advertisement after the Kentucky supreme 2 the letter as proposed by Shapiro. I think the answer to that is again emphatically "Yes, it can be banned."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that the issue before us? And isn't it properly before us?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: I think the issue is not properly before you, Justice White, because it was not raised in the Kentucky supreme court. What was raised in the Kentucky supreme court was a challenge to Rule 3.135. When the court adopted Model Rule 7.3, Petitioner had the right to seek rehearing or modification; or to begin a new proceeding. He did none of those. He simply sought certiorari from this Court. So we contend that by his failure to raise that issue in the Kentucky courts, he has deprived this Court of jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that seems rather strained to me, because he never knew about Rule 7.3 until the supreme court of Kentucky adopted it. And if 7.3 tracks in many respects the rule it was attacking, and during the course of the litigation, the authority -- the body with the authority to do so says, "Well, now this rule you're attacking is no longer in effect; but a new rule is," and he has the same complaint about that, doesn't strike you as rather highly a technical a thing to have to go back and start all over?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, it does not for these reasons: the old rule began, "A written advertisement may be sent." The new rule begins, "A lawyer may not solicit professional employment." In other words, it is the distinction between advertising on the one hand and solicitation on the other. This Court has said that a state can ban solicitation in Ohralik. Now, what Kentucky did was adopt without advance notice, admittedly, but what it did was adopt a new rule at the time Shapiro asked them to delete Rule 3.135.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the Kentucky supreme court disapprove this letter? It did, did it not?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: It did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It disapproved it, did it not?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And did it not disapprove it on the basis of the new rule?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then, why is the case here? That's what he's bringing here.

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: Well, our position is that that new rule should have first been challenged in the Kentucky supreme court rather than before this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what chance do you think a challenge would have had in the Kentucky supreme court after that court had adopted the rule?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And applied it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And applied it to this case?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: I can't get inside the mind of the Kentucky supreme court, but they, like other courts, are willing to listen and be educated; and if there was a challenge to the new rule, I think they would apply their wisdom and experience to it; and the facts that are presented to that court about the rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Doheny, it would be a different question if all the Kentucky Supreme Court did was to say, "You're right, the old rule is no good; we're adopting a new rule. " But it didn't just do that. You say that it went further, and in that situation, I can understand why you would say you would have to go back and see whether, under state law, this thing is banned under the new rule. But the court didn't stop there. It said, "We're adopting this new rule and your letter is banned under the new rule. "

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: Well, perhaps I —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did it say that or not? If it didn't say that, you have a different case.

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: Well, perhaps I overstated it, Mr. Justice Scalia, because it does not say in the opinion that 2 15 statement of advertising. And advertising is, by definition, Heritage is returned, what this letter says, for example, is "Call now. Don't wait." and the minute that call is returned, we have in- person solicitation by the lawyer, which this Court has said a state could properly prohibit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you could say the same thing of a newspaper ad -- "Call now. Don't wait." And the same result would ensue. Really, the only difference I see between the two is that one is economically more efficient than the other. Is there some vice in economically more efficient advertising, instead of -- you have the same ad; it reads exactly the same; and you say you can put it in the newspaper, but you can't mail it specifically to certain individuals?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: To targeted individuals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you call it "targeted." I'll call it "specific."

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: All right, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have to mail it to somebody.

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: You could mail it generally to those who do not -- are not known to have the problems, some of whom may.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a rather odd First Amendment doctrine that we forbid ads only when they're targeted at people that are interested in them, which is the purport of your argument

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: I'm trying, and am prepared to, draw a distinction between advertising on the one hand; and again, solicitation on the other. I suggest respectfully that there is a fundamental difference, and what Mr. Shapiro chose to do here is solicitation, to a specific individual, which I believe a state can, under the rulings of this Court in Qhralik properly prohibit because of the ability of the lawyer to overreach.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So basically, you're saying you can't be too effective?

turns.name: FRANK P. DOHENY, JR.
turns.text: I wouldn't word it that way, Justice Kennedy. I would say that some of the reasons against solicitations are -- there are numerous ones. We don't know if the person has a viable case; we don't know if the recipient has retained a lawyer; we don't know the mental or emotional state of the recipient when the solicitation arrives there. I think there are valid reasons that can result in the prohibition against solicitation, and the possibility or probability, whichever term you choose to use, of overreaching, is far greater on a specific communication than it is in a newspaper or a television advertisement, which you are free to reject rather easily. This Court has held -- and it's important to bear in mind that the Kentucky court did not reach its conclusion in a vacuum. It made a number of findings. It found in its opinion involving Shapiro, that dangers result from direct solicitation, whether in person or by mail. It stated that it was not unmindful of the potential for serious abuse with direct solicitation. It held that the submission of a blank form letter would not provide suitable protection for the public, and it stated that, under Kentucky rules, advertising was available to inform the public of the need for legal services, and the qualifications of those about to offer them without the risk that comes through impersonal solicitation. We, this morning -- some new lawyers were admitted as members of this Bar, and the Chief Justice welcomed them as Officers of this Court. This Court in Virginia Board of Pharmacy said "The state has a strong interest in maintaining professionalism among licensed pharmacists." If that is true with dispensers of a product, and surely it is; how much more true it must be with officers of the court who can invoke the powers of the court for good and for evil? We suggest that Kentucky was within its rights in adopting Model Rule 7.3, and that the decision of the Kentucky supreme court should be affirmed.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Doheny. The case is submitted.
